notes: Part 1 solved on first attempt (answer: 1168). Part 2 not yet unlocked. Circular dial 0-99 starting at 50, count landings on 0. Straightforward modular arithmetic simulation.

Both parts solved on first attempt. Part 1: count rotations ending at 0 (trivial simulation). Part 2: count all clicks through 0 mid-rotation — required deriving a closed-form formula (first crossing + every 100 steps), no brute-force stepping. Clean solution, no utils needed.

Both parts solved on first attempt. Problem: find numbers in ranges whose digit string is a repeated sequence. Key insight: a k-digit prefix n repeated m times equals n * (1 + 10^k + 10^(2k) + ... + 10^((m-1)k)). Part 1 (m=2 only) used multiplier 10^k+1. Part 2 (m>=2) iterated over all repetition counts per period length, deduplicating with a set. Required adding MustParseInt64 to utils. Generated all candidate numbers rather than checking each number in range — efficient even for large ranges.

Part 1 (pick 2 digits) solved on first attempt with brute-force pair iteration. Part 2 (pick 12 digits) required the greedy largest-k-subsequence algorithm: at each step, pick the max digit in the valid window [prev+1, n-k+i]. Failed once due to tie-breaking bug: using >= picked the rightmost max digit, but leftmost is required to preserve options for later picks. Fixed by switching to >. Clean generalisation: both parts use the same maxKJoltage function with k=2 and k=12.

Both parts solved on first attempt. Part 1: count '@' cells with fewer than 4 '@' neighbors (8-directional). Part 2: iterative removal — each round remove all currently accessible rolls, repeat until no accessible rolls remain, count total removed. Key insight: removals are batched per round (all accessible in the current state are removed simultaneously), not one-by-one. Used [][]byte mutable grid for Part 2 simulation. utils.ReadLines used for parsing.

Both parts solved on first attempt. Part 1: linear scan of available IDs against inclusive ranges — straightforward membership check. Part 2: count all integers covered by the union of ranges — solved with sort-and-merge interval algorithm. Key observation: Part 2 ignores the second section entirely. No edge cases; clean separation between the two interpretations of the same input.

Both parts solved on first attempt. The challenge was parsing a wide columnar worksheet (3727 chars/row, 5 rows). Part 1: within each column band (separated by all-space columns), each row is one number — trim and parse. Part 2: same band detection, but each character column within a band is one number (non-space chars top-to-bottom = digits), read right-to-left across the band. Refactored to share grid loading, separator detection, and band traversal via a bandSolver function argument. Used int64 throughout to handle large products.

Part 1 (411): BFS over 2^N XOR-toggle light states. Part 2 (16063): non-negative integer LP — min Σx_j s.t. Ax=b, x≥0 integer — solved via branch-and-bound with two-phase simplex relaxation. Three sequential bugs found and fixed: (1) used math.Round instead of B&B for fractional LP solutions, giving wrong low totals; (2) UB constraints in B&B sub-problems added as equalities (fixing x=U) instead of inequalities with slack variables (x+s=U, s≥0), causing wrong LP sub-problems; (3) degenerate Phase-1 artificials (value=0) left in basis before Phase 2, corrupting Phase 2 pivots and producing 'integer' solutions that violated equality constraints. Fix: drive out degenerate artificials before Phase 2, and verify candidate integer solutions against Ay=b. Notable: required implementing two-phase simplex with UB slack handling from scratch — a complex LP engineering task for a competitive programming problem.

Part 1 (523): count all directed paths from 'you' to 'out' in a DAG — textbook memoized DFS, paths('out')=1, paths(n)=sum(paths(child)). Part 2 (517315308154944): count paths from 'svr' to 'out' that visit both 'dac' and 'fft'. Extended memoisation state to (node, haveDac, haveFft) — a 3-tuple giving 4x state space, still O(N) time. Base case: paths('out', true, true)=1, else 0. Both parts solved first attempt with no wrong answers. Clean structural extension from scalar to tuple memoisation key.

Polyomino packing with 3x3 shapes. Key insight: all shapes have 3x3 bounding boxes. For the actual input, every region that passes the cell count check (total '#' cells <= W*H) also satisfies presents <= floor(W/3)*floor(H/3) aligned slots, making them trivially packable without backtracking. 519 regions fail cell count immediately; 481 pass and are all feasible. Backtracking fallback exists for example-like edge cases. Part 2 not yet available.

Part 1 (1642): tachyon beam splits at '^' splitters — beams at the same column merge (set semantics). Simulated row-by-row with a set of active columns. Part 2 (47274292756692): counts are multiplicative not boolean — beams at the same column accumulate, so each timeline is independent. Only change: set → map[int]int, propagating counts instead of presence. Clean one-attempt solution; the set→multiset insight was immediate. Duration estimated from file timestamps.

Part 1 (32103): Kruskal-style union-find connecting the 1000 closest pairs by Euclidean distance; product of top-3 component sizes. Part 2 (8133642976): find the LAST edge that merges all boxes into a single component; return product of the two nodes' X-coordinates. Required careful reading — Part 1 uses a fixed edge count (1000), Part 2 continues until fully connected and returns a different quantity entirely. Two logical sections reuse the same UF structure cleanly. Duration and attempt count estimated from file timestamps (main.go last modified ~57 min after input appeared).

Part 1 (4771508457): brute-force O(n^2) over all pairs of red tiles as rectangle corners; area = (|dx|+1)*(|dy|+1). Part 2 (1539809693): rectangle must lie entirely within the polygon. Solution: coordinate-compress x/y coords into an odd-indexed grid (even indices = gaps between tiles), trace polygon boundary on compressed grid, BFS flood-fill to mark all exterior cells, build 2D prefix sum of exterior cells, then for each pair check that the query rectangle contains zero exterior cells. Substantial complexity for Part 2 — coordinate compression with parity indexing is a non-trivial technique. Duration and ~3-attempt estimate from 69-minute window and code length.
